items.prompt: A routine removes every node whose value equals target. The original head may be removed repeatedly. Which setup most directly avoids a separate head-deletion branch?
constraints: The operation is in place and may change the logical head.
The list is singly linked and acyclic.

options.text: Sort payload values before traversal.
Create dummy.next = head and start prev at dummy.
Start fast two nodes ahead of slow.
Keep only a tail pointer and discard head.

feedback.reason: A sentinel gives the original head the same predecessor relationship as every later node.
feedback.details: With prev at dummy, deleting the current first data node uses the same assignment prev.next = current.next used elsewhere. Repeated head removals remain uniform because prev can stay at dummy. The final logical head is dummy.next. Relative-speed pointers, sorting, and a lone tail do not provide a stable predecessor for mutation.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.fast_slow: Different traversal speeds do not create a predecessor before a removable head.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_values: Sorting changes content order and does not eliminate the head-reference update problem.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.tail_only: A tail reference cannot update or recover the first link of a singly linked list.

items.prompt: A sentinel-based filter traverses current = prev.next. Select all statements that preserve the uniform predecessor invariant.
constraints: current is recomputed as prev.next after each transition.

options.text: When current is removed, advance prev to the removed node.
Return dummy itself as the first logical data node.
When current is removed, set prev.next = current.next and keep prev in place.
When current is retained, advance prev to current.

feedback.reason: Deletion keeps the same predecessor for the next candidate, while retention makes the kept node the next predecessor.
feedback.details: current is always read from prev.next. After deletion, prev.next changes to the successor, so prev still precedes the next candidate and must not move into the removed node. After retention, current remains in the result and becomes the correct predecessor. The result begins at dummy.next, not dummy. These transitions are what remove the special branch for the original head.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.delete_advance_prev: Advancing into a removed node breaks the invariant and may skip consecutive matching nodes.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.return_dummy: The sentinel is implementation scaffolding; returning it violates the stated logical-list contract.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.delete_keep_prev: Keeping prev is necessary to test the new successor after a deletion, especially for consecutive removals.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.retain_advance_prev: Advancing after retention preserves prev as the last kept node and predecessor of the next candidate.

items.prompt: A routine creates dummy.next = head and returns dummy.next after deleting the first matching node. Which pair of outcomes is correct?
constraints: At most one matching node is deleted.
The list is acyclic and the sentinel is not logical data.

options.text: If head is null, return null; if head is the match, set dummy.next = head.next and return that successor.
Return the sentinel node itself for both the empty and head-deletion cases.
Throw for empty input and always return null after deleting the head.
Allocate a new head node even when a successor already exists.

feedback.reason: The sentinel's next field is the canonical logical-head reference for both the empty and head-deletion cases.
feedback.details: When head is null, dummy.next is null and no mutation is needed. When the old head is removed, assigning dummy.next to its successor updates the logical head through the same predecessor link used for internal deletion. Returning dummy would expose scaffolding; returning null unconditionally would lose a nonempty suffix; replacement allocation is unnecessary.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.empty_dummy_head_dummy: The output contract excludes the sentinel, so dummy itself is not a valid logical result.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.empty_error_head_null: Empty input can be handled normally, and deleting the head does not imply that the entire suffix is empty.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.allocate_replacement: The existing successor already has the correct identity and link structure.

items.prompt: Two implementations are identical except one dummy stores -1 and the other stores 999. Both return dummy.next after constructing the same data-node chain. Under a sentinel-excluding contract, how do their logical outputs compare?
constraints: The public list model excludes sentinels.

options.text: They are the same logical list because dummy.value is outside the returned chain.
One result begins with -1 and the other with 999.
A dummy payload must be unique among all data payloads or the list is invalid.
Every data node receives a new identity based on dummy.value.

feedback.reason: The returned reference bypasses the sentinel, so its payload does not participate in the logical sequence.
feedback.details: A sentinel's payload is often arbitrary because the algorithm uses only its stable identity and next field. Returning dummy.next exposes the data-node chain, which is stated to be identical in both implementations. A sentinel value need not be unique because node identity—not payload—is used to distinguish it internally. The conclusion changes only if the representation exposes the sentinel as a logical member.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.different_first_value: That would be true only if the function returned dummy rather than dummy.next.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.different_identities: A predecessor payload does not replace or re-identify successor node objects.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.invalid_duplicate: Sentinel correctness does not depend on a payload value absent from the data domain when identity is available.

items.prompt: dummy.next = head. Starting with prev = dummy, a routine follows prev = prev.next k times so prev becomes the predecessor of zero-based position k. No auxiliary index exists. What are the time and extra-space costs?
constraints: The list is singly linked and acyclic.
Only dummy, head, and k are initially available.

feedback.reason: The sentinel gives a convenient starting predecessor but every later predecessor is still reached by sequential next traversal.
feedback.details: dummy makes position zero have a predecessor at zero traversal cost. For position k, prev follows k links from dummy, so time is O(k). Only prev and a counter are needed, giving O(1) extra space. An external array or map of node references could provide different lookup behavior, but the sentinel alone is not such an index.

items.prompt: dummy.value = 0, and the first data node D also has value 0. Which test safely distinguishes the sentinel from D inside the routine?
constraints: Zero is a legal data payload.
The routine retains the exact dummy reference it allocated.

options.text: Compare the node reference with the stored dummy reference.
Treat every node with value 0 as the sentinel.
Compare the values of their successor nodes.
Assume whichever zero-valued node is visited first is always logical data.

feedback.reason: The sentinel is one specific object, so its stored reference remains decisive even when payload values overlap.
feedback.details: Using a magic payload to identify a sentinel restricts the legal data domain and fails as soon as a data node contains that value. The routine already owns the dummy reference and can compare object identity directly. Successor values and visitation guesses do not establish role or identity. An explicit tagged node type could also work, but that is a different representation contract.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.value_not_zero: This misclassifies legitimate zero-valued data nodes and confuses payload with identity.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.position_guess: Traversal position can change during rewiring and is not a robust identity criterion.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.next_value: Successor payloads do not identify the current node object or its sentinel role.

items.prompt: prev may be dummy or a data node, and prev.next is the node to delete. No other reference preserves the successor. Order the transition while keeping prev ready to examine the next candidate.
constraints: prev.next is non-null and is the selected victim.
Consecutive candidates may also need deletion.

elements.text: Keep prev unchanged for the next candidate.

feedback.reason: The victim and successor are captured before the predecessor link changes, and deletion leaves prev at the same predecessor.
feedback.details: The sequence is identical whether prev is dummy or a data node, which is the sentinel's benefit. Capturing successor before bypassing prevents loss of the remaining chain. Keeping prev unchanged is essential when consecutive nodes may be deleted; advancing to victim would move into an unlinked node and skip the new prev.next candidate.

items.prompt: Select all tasks where a sentinel directly removes a material head or first-attachment special case under the stated contracts.
constraints: Sentinel usefulness means it removes a real branch or missing-anchor condition.
The sentinel remains outside the logical result.

options.text: Find a middle node in a read-only traversal with a fixed even-length convention.
Build a merged output when the first selected node is not known in advance.
Remove matching nodes when the original head may be deleted repeatedly.
Detect whether next traversal enters a cycle without mutating links.

feedback.reason: Deletion needs a uniform predecessor and merge construction needs a stable initial tail; the read-only traversal tasks do not have those boundary problems.
feedback.details: For repeated head deletion, dummy acts as the predecessor that remains before the changing logical head. For merge construction, dummy is an anchor whose next becomes the first output node. Cycle detection and middle finding rely on traversal identity or phase; adding a sentinel does not supply their proof and can shift initialization if used carelessly.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.detect_cycle_boolean: Cycle existence is decided by traversal topology and reference convergence, not by a missing predecessor.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.find_middle_readonly: Read-only middle finding needs a speed-phase convention, not a writable anchor before head.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.remove_matching_heads: This is the canonical changing-head boundary that a sentinel unifies.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.build_merged_output: A dummy tail removes the special case for attaching the first selected output node.
